title: Pre-Meeting Background Brief
prompt:
I'm meeting with [NAME] from [COMPANY] tomorrow. Research their background and give me a brief that includes:
- Current role and responsibilities
- Career history (last 3 positions)
- Education and notable achievements
- Recent public activity (blog posts, talks, tweets)
- Shared connections or interests we might have
- 3 conversation starters based on their interests

Keep it concise — one page max.

title: Social Profile Discovery
prompt:
Find all public social profiles for [NAME] who works at [COMPANY] as [ROLE]. Look for:
- LinkedIn profile URL
- Twitter/X handle
- GitHub username
- Personal website or blog
- Any podcast appearances or conference talks

For each profile found, rate your confidence (high/medium/low) that it's the right person. Note any ambiguity.

title: Company Context Research
prompt:
Give me context on [COMPANY] that would be useful for understanding [NAME]'s work environment:
- What does the company do? (one paragraph)
- Company size, funding stage, recent news
- Tech stack if relevant
- Company culture signals (Glassdoor themes, LinkedIn posts)
- Key competitors and market position

Focus on what's relevant for building a working relationship.

title: Mutual Connection Finder
prompt:
I work at [MY COMPANY] and I'm trying to connect with [NAME] at [THEIR COMPANY]. Help me find warm introduction paths:
- Look for shared LinkedIn connections
- Shared alumni networks (schools, previous companies)
- Shared community memberships (Slack groups, open source, conferences)
- Shared interests or hobbies visible from public profiles

Suggest the 3 strongest paths for a warm introduction.

title: Expertise Map
prompt:
Based on [NAME]'s public profile, GitHub activity, and any published content, map their expertise:
- Primary domain expertise (what they're known for)
- Technical skills and languages
- Topics they write/speak about
- Areas they seem to be learning or exploring
- How they position themselves professionally

Present as a structured profile I can reference quickly.

title: Digital Footprint Summary
prompt:
Create a comprehensive digital footprint summary for [NAME]:
- All public profiles and accounts found
- Publishing activity (blogs, Medium, Substack, dev.to)
- Open source contributions
- Forum activity (HN, Reddit, Stack Overflow)
- Speaking engagements or podcast appearances
- Estimated online influence and reach

Note: only use publicly available information.

title: Role & Responsibility Inference
prompt:
Based on [NAME]'s LinkedIn profile and public information, infer their likely:
- Day-to-day responsibilities
- Team size they manage (if applicable)
- Key stakeholders they work with
- Decision-making authority level
- Current priorities based on recent activity

This helps me understand what matters to them in conversations.

title: Interest & Hobby Profiler
prompt:
From [NAME]'s social media and public profiles, identify personal interests and hobbies:
- Sports, fitness, outdoor activities
- Books, podcasts, media they follow
- Side projects or passion projects
- Causes they support or advocate for
- Travel or location-related interests

These are conversation starters — focus on genuine signals, not assumptions.

title: Communication Style Analysis
prompt:
Analyze the following Slack messages from [NAME] and characterize their communication style:

[PASTE MESSAGES]

Assess:
- Formality level (casual ↔ formal)
- Message length preference (brief ↔ detailed)
- Emoji/reaction usage patterns
- How they give feedback (direct ↔ diplomatic)
- How they ask for help
- Response time patterns if visible
- Overall energy (enthusiastic ↔ measured)

Give me a one-paragraph summary I can use to adapt my communication.

title: Working Style Profile
prompt:
Based on these Slack messages and meeting transcripts from [NAME], profile their working style:

[PASTE CONTENT]

Analyze:
- Decision-making approach (data-driven ↔ intuition)
- Collaboration preference (async ↔ sync)
- How they handle disagreement
- Planning style (structured ↔ flexible)
- What motivates them (recognition, autonomy, impact, mastery)
- Stress indicators and how they cope

Format as a practical guide for working well with this person.

title: Meeting Behavior Analysis
prompt:
Analyze this Zoom transcript involving [NAME] and describe their meeting behavior:

[PASTE TRANSCRIPT]

Look for:
- How much they speak vs. listen
- Do they facilitate or follow?
- How do they react to new ideas?
- Do they summarize or ask clarifying questions?
- Body language cues mentioned (if video call notes)
- How they close or action items

Help me understand how to have more effective meetings with them.

title: DISC Profile Estimation
prompt:
Based on the following messages and interactions from [NAME], estimate their DISC personality profile:

[PASTE CONTENT]

For each dimension, rate High/Medium/Low and provide evidence:
- Dominance (D): Results-oriented, decisive, competitive
- Influence (I): Enthusiastic, collaborative, optimistic
- Steadiness (S): Patient, reliable, team-oriented
- Conscientiousness (C): Analytical, detail-oriented, systematic

Suggest the best communication approach based on their profile.

title: Conversation Starter Generator
prompt:
Based on this profile of [NAME]:

[PASTE PROFILE/MESSAGES]

Generate 10 natural conversation starters that would resonate with them. Mix:
- 3 work-related topics they'd be passionate about
- 3 based on personal interests or hobbies
- 2 based on recent activity or news
- 2 thoughtful questions that show genuine curiosity

Avoid anything that feels stalker-ish. These should feel natural.

title: Conflict Style Assessment
prompt:
Analyze how [NAME] handles disagreements based on these message threads:

[PASTE THREADS]

Identify:
- Their default conflict response (avoid, accommodate, compete, compromise, collaborate)
- Language patterns when stressed or frustrated
- De-escalation techniques they use
- What triggers defensiveness
- How to constructively disagree with them

Give me practical advice for navigating difficult conversations.

title: Values & Priorities Extraction
prompt:
From these messages and interactions from [NAME], extract their core professional values and priorities:

[PASTE CONTENT]

Identify:
- What they consistently advocate for
- What frustrates them (reveals what they value)
- How they spend their discretionary time
- What they celebrate or praise in others
- Their definition of "good work"

Present as a values hierarchy with evidence.

title: Feedback Style Guide
prompt:
Based on [NAME]'s communication patterns in these messages:

[PASTE CONTENT]

Create a guide for giving them feedback effectively:
- Preferred format (written vs verbal, 1:1 vs public)
- How direct can you be?
- Do they prefer examples or principles?
- How do they typically receive criticism?
- Best time/context for feedback conversations
- Phrases that resonate vs phrases to avoid

Make this actionable for their next review or 1:1.

title: Energy & Motivation Map
prompt:
Analyze [NAME]'s messages over the past month and map their energy and motivation:

[PASTE MESSAGES]

Track:
- Topics that energize them (enthusiastic language, quick responses)
- Topics that drain them (short responses, delayed replies)
- Time-of-day patterns
- Day-of-week patterns
- Projects that generate the most positive language
- Signs of burnout or disengagement

Visualize as an energy map I can reference.

title: Learning Style Identifier
prompt:
From [NAME]'s Slack activity and GitHub contributions, identify their learning style:

[PASTE ACTIVITY]

Assess:
- Do they learn by doing, reading, or discussing?
- How do they approach new technologies?
- Do they share what they learn? How?
- What resources do they reference (docs, videos, peers)?
- How do they handle being stuck?

Suggest development opportunities that match their learning style.

title: 1:1 Briefing Document
prompt:
Prepare a 1:1 briefing for my meeting with [NAME] ([ROLE]):

Context: [RELATIONSHIP CONTEXT]

Include:
1. Quick bio refresh (role, tenure, recent projects)
2. Their recent Slack activity themes (what's on their mind)
3. Any blockers or frustrations they've mentioned
4. Topics they might want to discuss
5. 3 questions I should ask
6. Follow-ups from our last interaction

Keep it to one page, scannable format.

title: Client Meeting Prep Pack
prompt:
I have a meeting with [CLIENT NAME] from [COMPANY] about [TOPIC]. Prepare a meeting prep pack:

1. Attendee profiles (brief background on each person)
2. Company context (recent news, priorities, challenges)
3. Relationship history (previous interactions, deals, concerns)
4. Their likely questions and my suggested answers
5. My questions for them (prioritized)
6. Desired outcome and fallback positions
7. Follow-up actions to propose

Format for quick scanning 5 minutes before the call.

title: Team Meeting Attendee Cheat Sheet
prompt:
I'm joining a team meeting with these people: [LIST NAMES AND ROLES]

For each person, give me:
- Their role and what they own
- Communication style in one line
- What they care about most
- How to get their buy-in
- One thing to avoid

Format as a table I can glance at during the meeting.

title: Skip-Level 1:1 Prep
prompt:
I'm having a skip-level 1:1 with [NAME] who reports to [MANAGER]. Prepare me:

1. Their current projects and responsibilities
2. Recent wins to acknowledge
3. Any signals of frustration or disengagement from Slack
4. Their career trajectory and aspirations (if known)
5. Questions that feel genuine, not interrogative:
   - About their growth
   - About team dynamics
   - About what I could do better as a leader
6. Topics to avoid or handle carefully

Make this feel human, not corporate.

title: Board Meeting People Brief
prompt:
Prepare attendee briefs for our board meeting. For each board member:

[LIST NAMES]

Include:
- Background and expertise area
- What they typically focus on in board meetings
- Questions they're likely to ask
- How to frame updates for their perspective
- Recent interactions or communications
- Hot buttons to be aware of

Format as individual cards, one per board member.

title: Interview Panel Prep
prompt:
I'm interviewing [CANDIDATE NAME] for [ROLE]. Prepare an interview brief:

1. Resume highlights and red flags
2. LinkedIn/GitHub analysis
3. Questions tailored to their background
4. Culture fit signals to look for
5. Technical areas to probe based on their experience
6. Comparison to our ideal candidate profile
7. Things they might ask us (and good answers)

Help me run a thorough but human interview.

title: Difficult Conversation Prep
prompt:
I need to have a difficult conversation with [NAME] about [TOPIC].

Based on their communication profile:
[PASTE PROFILE OR CONTEXT]

Help me prepare:
1. Opening statement (direct but empathetic)
2. Key points to make (prioritized)
3. Anticipated reactions and how to respond
4. Questions to ask to understand their perspective
5. Specific language to use / avoid based on their style
6. Desired outcome and compromise positions
7. How to close constructively

Tone: [SPECIFY - firm/gentle/collaborative]

title: Networking Event Prep
prompt:
I'm attending [EVENT NAME] and want to connect with these people:

[LIST NAMES AND COMPANIES]

For each person:
1. Quick background (30-second mental model)
2. Why I want to connect (my angle)
3. Why they'd want to connect (their angle)
4. Ice-breaker question specific to them
5. Follow-up action to propose

Also suggest 3 general conversation starters for the event theme.

title: Vendor Meeting Intelligence
prompt:
We're meeting with [VENDOR] to discuss [PRODUCT/SERVICE]. Research the people attending:

[LIST NAMES AND ROLES]

For each:
- Their role in the sales process (decision maker, technical, executive sponsor)
- LinkedIn background and tenure at company
- Communication style prediction
- What motivates them (commission, relationship, technical excellence)

Also: recent company news, competitor landscape, and our leverage points.

title: Personalized LinkedIn Connection
prompt:
Write a LinkedIn connection request to [NAME] ([ROLE] at [COMPANY]).

Context about them:
[PASTE PROFILE SUMMARY]

Context about me:
[MY ROLE AND COMPANY]

Reason for connecting: [REASON]

Requirements:
- Under 300 characters
- Reference something specific from their profile
- No generic "I'd love to connect" language
- Clear but not pushy about why
- Professional but warm tone

title: Cold Email — Warm Angle
prompt:
Write a cold outreach email to [NAME] at [COMPANY]. Find a warm angle using this profile data:

[PASTE PROFILE/RESEARCH]

Purpose: [WHAT I WANT]

Rules:
- Subject line that gets opened (reference their work, not mine)
- First line that proves I did my homework
- One paragraph max on what I'm proposing
- Clear, low-friction CTA
- Under 150 words total
- No "I hope this email finds you well"
- Write 3 variations with different angles

title: Follow-Up After Meeting
prompt:
Write a follow-up email after meeting [NAME] at [EVENT/CONTEXT].

What we discussed: [TOPICS]
Their interests: [FROM PROFILE]
What I promised to share: [ITEMS]
Next step I want: [DESIRED ACTION]

Requirements:
- Reference a specific moment from our conversation
- Include the promised resource/link
- Suggest a concrete next step with a specific time
- Keep it under 100 words
- Tone: [professional/casual/enthusiastic]

title: Introduction Email
prompt:
Write an introduction email connecting [PERSON A] and [PERSON B].

Person A context: [BACKGROUND]
Person B context: [BACKGROUND]
Why they should meet: [REASON]

Format:
- Brief context on each person (2 lines max)
- Why I think they'd benefit from connecting
- Suggest what they could discuss
- Make it easy for either to opt out gracefully
- BCC me after they reply

title: Slack DM — First Contact
prompt:
Write a Slack DM to [NAME] who I haven't interacted with before.

Their profile: [ROLE, TEAM, INTERESTS]
Why I'm reaching out: [REASON]
What I need: [ASK]

Rules:
- Don't start with "Hey, I know we haven't talked before"
- Lead with context or a compliment about their work
- Be specific about the ask
- Make it easy to say no
- Appropriate for Slack tone (not too formal)
- Under 4 short paragraphs

title: Re-Engagement Message
prompt:
Write a re-engagement message to [NAME] whom I haven't spoken to in [TIME PERIOD].

Last interaction: [CONTEXT]
Their recent activity: [FROM PROFILE/SOCIAL]
Why I'm reaching out now: [REASON]

Requirements:
- Acknowledge the time gap naturally
- Reference something they've done recently (shows I've been paying attention)
- Don't be apologetic about losing touch
- Have a genuine reason, not just "checking in"
- Suggest something specific and easy

title: Conference Speaker Outreach
prompt:
Write an outreach message inviting [NAME] to speak at [EVENT].

Their expertise: [FROM PROFILE]
Event context: [DETAILS]
Why them specifically: [REASON]

Include:
- Specific topic suggestion based on their recent work
- What's in it for them (audience, exposure, connections)
- Logistics (date, format, time commitment)
- Social proof (previous speakers, attendee count)
- Easy yes/no CTA

title: Thank You Note Generator
prompt:
Write a genuine thank-you note to [NAME] for [WHAT THEY DID].

Context: [RELATIONSHIP AND SITUATION]
Their personality: [COMMUNICATION STYLE]

Requirements:
- Specific about what they did and why it mattered
- Match their communication style (formal/casual)
- Don't over-do it — authentic over effusive
- Include a forward-looking element
- Medium: [email/Slack/handwritten note]
- Length appropriate for the medium

title: Referral Request
prompt:
Write a message to [NAME] asking for a referral for [ROLE] at [COMPANY].

My background: [BRIEF SUMMARY]
Our relationship: [HOW WE KNOW EACH OTHER]
Why this role: [GENUINE REASON]

Rules:
- Don't make it transactional
- Make it easy for them (attach resume, include key points)
- Acknowledge that it's an ask
- Give them an easy out
- Offer to reciprocate somehow
- Include 3 bullet points they could forward to the hiring manager

title: Team Communication Network
prompt:
Analyze these Slack message patterns for our team and map the communication network:

[PASTE MESSAGE DATA OR DESCRIBE CHANNELS]

Identify:
- Communication hubs (who connects different groups)
- Isolated members (low interaction count)
- Strongest bilateral relationships
- Cross-functional bridges
- Information bottlenecks

Visualize as a network description and recommend actions to improve connectivity.

title: Team Health Check
prompt:
Based on the past month of Slack messages from our team, perform a health check:

[PASTE OR DESCRIBE ACTIVITY]

Assess:
- Overall sentiment trend (improving/stable/declining)
- Engagement levels per person
- Response time patterns
- After-hours activity (potential burnout)
- Celebration vs complaint ratio
- Collaboration patterns

Flag any individuals who might need attention. Suggest 3 team-level interventions.

title: Collaboration Pattern Analysis
prompt:
Analyze collaboration patterns for [TEAM NAME] across Slack and GitHub:

[PASTE ACTIVITY DATA]

Map:
- Who works together most frequently
- Async vs sync collaboration preferences by person
- Code review relationships (who reviews whose PRs)
- Channel participation patterns
- Meeting vs messaging preferences

Identify collaboration gaps and suggest pairing optimizations.

title: Onboarding Progress Tracker
prompt:
[NEW HIRE NAME] joined [TEAM] on [DATE]. Based on their Slack and GitHub activity, assess their onboarding progress:

[PASTE ACTIVITY]

Track:
- Channels joined and participation level
- Questions asked (frequency and topics)
- Who they interact with most
- First code contributions
- Integration into team rituals
- Confidence indicators (message tone, question complexity)

Compare to typical onboarding milestones and flag any concerns.

title: Meeting Load Analysis
prompt:
Analyze meeting patterns for our team:

[PASTE CALENDAR/MEETING DATA]

Calculate:
- Total meeting hours per person per week
- Meeting-free blocks available
- Recurring vs ad-hoc ratio
- Average attendee count
- Meetings that could be async
- People who are over-meetinged

Recommend specific meetings to cut, combine, or convert to async.

title: Knowledge Distribution Map
prompt:
Map knowledge distribution across our team based on their activity:

[PASTE GITHUB REPOS, SLACK CHANNELS, DOCS]

Identify:
- Single points of failure (bus factor = 1)
- Knowledge silos
- Well-distributed expertise areas
- Emerging experts vs established experts
- Documentation gaps (knowledge exists in heads, not docs)

Prioritize knowledge-sharing actions by risk level.

title: Team Dynamics Report
prompt:
Generate a team dynamics report from these Slack interactions:

[PASTE MESSAGES FROM TEAM CHANNELS]

Analyze:
- Power dynamics (who influences decisions)
- Psychological safety indicators (do people disagree openly?)
- Recognition patterns (who praises whom)
- Decision-making speed and style
- Humor and social bonding signals
- Subgroup formations

Present as an executive summary with specific, actionable recommendations.

title: Cross-Team Collaboration Score
prompt:
Score cross-team collaboration between [TEAM A] and [TEAM B]:

[PASTE INTERACTION DATA]

Metrics:
- Frequency of cross-team messages
- Shared channel participation
- Joint project involvement
- Cross-team PR reviews
- Meeting attendance overlap
- Escalation vs collaboration ratio

Score 1-10 with benchmarks. Identify the top cross-team connectors and suggest ways to strengthen the bridge.

title: Burnout Risk Assessment
prompt:
Assess burnout risk for team members based on activity patterns:

[PASTE ACTIVITY DATA - SLACK, GITHUB, CALENDAR]

Red flags to check:
- After-hours message frequency (trending up?)
- Weekend activity
- Shortened message length over time
- Decreased emoji/reaction usage
- Fewer social interactions
- Increased response latency
- PTO usage (or lack thereof)

Rank each person's risk level and suggest personalized interventions. Be sensitive — this is about care, not surveillance.

title: Team Skill Gap Analysis
prompt:
Based on our team's GitHub activity, Slack discussions, and roles, perform a skill gap analysis:

Team members: [LIST WITH ROLES]
Upcoming projects: [LIST PROJECTS AND TECH REQUIREMENTS]

Map:
- Current team skills inventory
- Required skills for upcoming work
- Gaps between current and needed
- Who is closest to filling each gap
- Training recommendations per person
- Hiring needs if gaps can't be filled internally

Prioritize by project timeline urgency.
